keyEvents.event: Free Officers revolution topples the monarchy
keyEvents.impact: Established the army as the permanent core of the Egyptian state under Nasser
Nationalizing the canal and surviving Anglo-French-Israeli attack made Egypt the leader of Arab nationalism
Catastrophic loss of Sinai shattered Nasserist prestige and the pan-Arab project

keyEvents.event: October War crossing of the Suez Canal
keyEvents.impact: Restored military honor and created the leverage for negotiated return of Sinai

keyEvents.event: Camp David peace treaty with Israel
keyEvents.impact: Traded the Arab leadership mantle for Sinai and massive US military aid; cold peace ever since
Mass revolt showed the street could topple a pharaoh; brief Muslim Brotherhood rule followed
Military reasserted control, crushed the Brotherhood, and rebuilt a harder security state

history.narrative: The Arab world's demographic heavyweight, ruled by a military establishment that prizes stability over ambition and lives off Suez Canal rents, Gulf bailouts, and American aid. Its strategic horizons are defensive: the Nile's waters, Sinai's security, and keeping chaos in Libya, Sudan, and Gaza from spilling across its borders.

redLines.description: Threats to Nile water security, including unilateral upstream dam operations
Loss of control over Sinai or mass displacement from Gaza into Egyptian territory
Closure or attack on the Suez Canal

internalDivisions.name: Suppressed Muslim Brotherhood and Islamist opposition
Coptic Christian minority facing sectarian violence

goals.description: Secure Nile water rights against upstream dam projects
Stabilize debt and currency through Gulf investment and IMF support
Crush the Sinai insurgency and insulate Egypt from Gaza and Libya spillover
Reclaim leadership of the Arab world as its indispensable mediator
